ai_summary: Elon Musk announced Starlink's expansion into Lesotho, a Southern African nation, signaling geographic expansion of SpaceX's satellite internet service. This represents a concrete business milestone in Starlink's global rollout strategy to provide connectivity to underserved markets, demonstrating progress in the company's mission to achieve worldwide broadband coverage and expand its addressable market beyond developed nations.
embed_html:
Starlink in Lesotho! 🇱🇸 https://t.co/KAPoUs3j1D

— Elon Musk (@elonmusk) June 20, 2026

summary: Q1 tonnage to orbit by launch provider. Once Starship is flying hourly, SpaceX’s mass to orbit will be about 100 times more than everyone else combined, even if they triple their current launch rate.
ai_summary: Elon Musk provides operational metrics on SpaceX's launch cadence, projecting that once Starship achieves hourly flight rates, SpaceX will deliver approximately 100x more mass to orbit than all other launch providers combined—even if competitors triple their current capacity. This demonstrates the transformative scale advantage of SpaceX's reusable rocket technology and illustrates how revolutionary manufacturing/operational improvements can create near-insurmountable competitive moats in the space launch market.

ai_summary: SpaceX received a Baa1 investment-grade credit rating from Moody's (higher than Tesla's Baa3), citing its exceptional franchise strength as the world's leading orbital launch provider. Elon disputes Tesla's lower rating, noting the company holds $40B+ in cash, carries no debt, and maintains consistent profitability despite leading the EV market and diversifying into batteries and robotics—highlighting a potential disconnect between credit agencies' assessment and Tesla's actual financial strength and market position.
embed_html:
Yeah, makes no sense.

Tesla has over $40B in cash, no debt and is consistently profitable!

— Elon Musk (@elonmusk) June 19, 2026

SpaceX has just officially received an investment grade credit rating of Baa1 from Moody's, slightly higher than @Tesla's Baa3 rating.

Their reasoning: "SpaceX's Baa1 issuer rating reflects the company's exceptional franchise strength as the world's leading orbital launch… pic.twitter.com/ZXquJQEKYq

ai_summary: Moody's assigned SpaceX a Baa1 investment-grade credit rating, one notch higher than Tesla's Baa3 rating, citing SpaceX's exceptional franchise strength as the world's leading orbital launch provider. Elon's response highlights a business strategy insight: Tesla's credit rating appears artificially depressed relative to its market position and operational performance, suggesting either market undervaluation of Tesla's business fundamentals or an opportunity to improve financial metrics and investor confidence.
embed_html:
Tesla’s credit rating is ridiculously low tbh

— Elon Musk (@elonmusk) June 19, 2026
